*Magic Stick diaper ointment [[ASIN:B002RT8OQE Magic Stick All Natural Diaper Ointment, Clear, Net Wt. 2 oz.]]
*Brabantia Pedal Bin[[ASIN:B000MRTB06 Pedal Bin, 30 Litre (White) (25.98"H x 11.54"W x 15.55"D)]]
*36 Grovia Wipes [[ASIN:B003X3R6TO GroVia Cotton Cloth Wipes, 12 count]]
*Diaper Detergent [[ASIN:B002IA8MO6 Tiny Bubbles Detergent, Net Wt. 60 oz., Yellow Box]]

Non-essentials, we use these items for convenience,
*Diaper scraper [[ASIN:B0052WJ1NI Make My Day Two Birds Silicone Spatula and Scraper Set, Black]]
*Diaper sprayer [[ASIN:B000ZKHVMU bumGenius Diaper Sprayer]]
*Wipe Warmer (perfectly fits grovia wipes) [[ASIN:B001R95J2W Prince Lionheart Premium Wipe Warmer]]
*2 waterproof changing pad covers [[ASIN:B002UD660S Carters Keep Me Dry Flannel Bassinet Pad, Ecru]]
*24 washcloths (for top layer of changing pad)Martex Hospitality Washcloths - 24-Pack (White)
*1 little pottyBABYBJÖRN Little Potty - White
*Disposable Biodegradable diapers for backup and travel Broody Chick 100% Natural Fully Compostable Diapers Jumbo Box (Junior 26.5 - 55.1 lbs. (96-Count))
*Disposable overnight diapers for heavy wetters Seventh Gen. - Baby Overnight Diapers, Stage 4, 24 Count (Pack of 4) = 96 total diapers
*Triple Paste Diaper Ointment for use with disposablesTriple Paste Medicated Ointment for Diaper Rash, 16-Ounce

There are a number of items that we have tried, but did not find useful and either returned, donated or gave away. There was quite a bit of trial and error and research that went into the above system. I have only listed the items that work beautifully and will simplify your cloth diapering efforts.

I will jump right in with some gripes.
Cloth diapering is not cheap and despite industry claims, I have not realized cost savings in the first year (as compared to buying premium disposable diapers at Costco). When you add up the cost of the diapers and accessories, the cost of your time doing all those extra loads of laundry (or paying a diaper service), the cost of the resources to wash and dry all those loads, and the cost of all the special "cloth friendly" accoutrements necessary to care for the diapers without ruining them, it really adds up. If you are going to use the system for more than once child, then you will realize some significant savings (be sure to buy gender-neutral covers if that is your intention). The bigger motivation to cloth diaper (at least for me) is the dramatically reduced environmental impact and health benefits of cloth diapering. Conventional disposable diapers are loaded with toxins that pollute the earth, the workers that produce them and of course, your baby. Not to mention that it takes over 500 years for regular disposables to break down in the environment. That means that your babies dirty diapers will still be polluting the earth when your great, great, great, great grandchildren are born. Talk about a crappy legacy to leave behind!

Now the good stuff.
I chose Grovia because of the convenience and simplicity of the whole system. I knew that if I was going to cloth diaper, it would need to be as simple and easy as possible, even if that meant I would pay a bit more up front. There is a massive amount of information about cloth diapering on the net and much of it is overly complicated. Cloth diapering does not have to be complicated. You can simplify things quite a bit by sticking with one brand/ one system.

Having said that, the grovia system was too big, hard and bulky for our 10 pound newborn (and Grovia products are pretty trim compared to most cloth diapering systems) so we used disposables for the first couple of months. It would look like he was sleeping on an incline because the grovia insert was so thick under his bum. Other cloth brands have special newborn sized diapers and inserts, but I chose not to invest the money for newborn cloth diapers that would only be used for a short time. We used Broody Chick 100% compostable disposable diapers for that period and the added convenience of the disposables was great during that initial difficult phase. We had some minor problems with diaper rash during that time, but the Triple Paste always cleared it up in a day (then we would go back to the Grovia Magic Stick).

I breastfeed our baby so the diaper changes are more frequent (than formula fed babies). I wanted enough diapers to last three days. I did not want to do an extra load of laundry every two days. I kept adding covers, inserts and wipes until I had enough to last three days, hence the numbers above. If I had known in the beginning how many covers and inserts were going to be required, I would have saved some money by buying some of the larger Grovia combo deals.

The 30 liter Brabantia garbage pail is all steel, easy to clean and self sealing (no stink outside the pail) and the perfect size for three days worth of Grovia wipes, covers, inserts and changing pads. It has an inner plastic pail with handle that makes trips to the laundry room a breeze. I reuse use the plastic liners (that came with the pail) by putting them in the sun and alternating every three days. The sun eliminates all the odor from the liners.

I primarily used the 6 velcro covers (more convenient than snaps) through 11 months when our baby was finally able to figure out how to open them (and they are pretty worn looking velcro tabs at this point). Then I transitioned into primarily using the 9 snap covers and keeping the velcro covers only as backup. Grovia has a service that will remove the velcro tabs and replace with snaps for $6 a diaper. I wish they would offer a service to just replace the velcro, but I am happy with the durability of ALL the covers. They have held up better than I expected.

Our son gets a lot of time in nature. We don't worry about his clothing or diapers getting dirty if he is having a great experience. The darker colors and the patterned shells hide dirt and grass soiling better than the light solid shells. The gentle cloth diaper detergents aren't great at getting out dirt or grass stains either. If I had to do it over, I would not buy any light colored solid covers.

We bought some Grovia Booster pads to try before we went to the disposable overnight diapers, but our guy would soak through the regular "Soaker Pad" and the Booster Pad and wake up with wet clothes and bedding. There just wasn't enough absorbency for our breast fed baby. Changing him several times a night just added to the stress of those early days so we transitioned over to the disposables for night time and have been happy with that choice ever since.

The Grovia Magic Stick is truly brilliant! It is all natural, effective and does not require you to dip your fingers into diaper ointment while wrangling a diaper onto a squirming baby several times a day! It also does not adversely affect the absorbency of cloth diapers they way regular ointments do. Do not use the Triple paste (or any regular diaper ointment) with your cloth diapers. The waxes and petroleum oils in regular diaper ointments coat the cotton fibers and prevent them from absorbing liquids. We tried several top rated "natural" ointments for our occasional diaper rash, but Triple Paste worked where the natural ones failed. We used it sparingly whenever bumps appeared (which was pretty rare compared to our disposable diapering friends). Here are the ingredients (which are not listed on Amazon at the time of this review):Grapeseed oil, Beeswax, Meadowfoam oil, Organic Jojoba oil, Shea oil, Organic rosehip and a proprietary blend of essential oils.

Grovia wipes are fantastic. I wrote a separate review on these that compared the three types of wipes I bought. Grovia wipes were the best of the three and they work with the Prince Lionheart wipe warmer perfectly.

Little Potty. We started our son on his Little Potty when he was about three months old. Whenever he would cue that he was "going", we would take him to his potty to go. Now at 12 months, we take him to his potty after every meal and when he cues and he uses the potty pretty consistently. It is easier to dump and rinse the little potty contents into the toilet than it is to change and wash his diapers so we encourage him to use his little potty as much as possible. He gets lots of positive feedback and interaction when he "makes it" to the potty so he enjoys using it. At 12 months, he is very comfortable sitting on a potty to pee or poo. We keep the Little Potty on the bathroom counter and stand with him while he is sitting on it. I have seen lots of people start out with the Little Potty on the floor, but if you have the counter space in your bathroom, this will save your knees and back and make it easier for you to interact with your baby while on the potty. In another month or two, we will transition it to the floor for his use.

Grovia Little Bubbles diaper detergent. I have used the 5 top rated diaper detergents and honestly, I can't tell a lick of difference between them. I do a prewash with 1/2 indicated amount detergent and then a full wash with extra rinse using the full amount (per detergent instructions) of detergent. The most important thing is that you use a detergent designed for cloth diapers. Regular detergents have enzymes that will deteriorate the waterproof barriers in your cloth diapers. Cloth diaper detergents also do not have the dyes and perfumes that can irritate baby skin. I am currently using Grovia detergent and it is at least as good as the other top rated diaper detergents. I can't say that it is anymore effective at removing stains than the others. My diaper inserts are stained, but they are clean, soft and have no smell whatsoever when they come out of the dryer. I have always used cloth diaper detergent and I have never had to "strip" them. I use 14 ounces of wool dryer balls to dry my inserts and wipes. The dryer balls shaved ten minutes off the 80 minute dry cycle required to get a load of diapers dry. It takes only 70 minutes with the added wool balls.

I will come back from time to time to update this review. I hope it was helpful for those trying to wade through all the cloth diapering info out there and find a simple set up!

We bought some Grovia Booster pads to try before we went to the disposable overnight diapers, but our guy would soak through the regular "Soaker Pad" and the Booster Pad and wake up with wet clothes and bedding. There just wasn't enough absorbency for our breast fed baby. Changing him several times a night just added to the stress of those early days so we transitioned over to the disposables for night time and have been happy with that choice ever since.

The Grovia Magic Stick is truly brilliant! It is all natural, effective and does not require you to dip your fingers into diaper ointment while wrangling a diaper onto a squirming baby several times a day! It also does not adversely affect the absorbency of cloth diapers they way regular diaper ointments do. Do not use the Badger Diaper Cream (or any regular diaper ointment) with your cloth diapers. The waxes and petroleum oils in regular diaper ointments coat the cotton fibers and prevent them from absorbing liquids. We tried several top rated "natural" ointments for our occasional diaper rash, but the Badger Ointment worked where the other ones failed. We used it sparingly whenever bumps appeared (which was pretty rare compared to our disposable diapering friends).

Grovia wipes are fantastic. I wrote a separate review on these that compared the three types of wipes I bought. Grovia wipes were the best of the three and they work with the Prince Lionheart wipe warmer perfectly.

I will come back from time to time to update this review. I hope it was helpful for those trying to wade through all the cloth diapering info out there and find a simple set up!

Bum Genius Diaper Sprayer. We bought this, but after reading stories of flimsy parts and flooding disasters, we replaced the hose and a couple other flimsy parts with higher quality metal plumbing pieces and have not had any problems for the past year.

I will come back from time to time to update this review. I hope it was helpful for those trying to wade through all the cloth diapering info out there and find a simple, but effective set up.

Thanks for reading!

*Disposable Biodegradable diapers for backup and travel Broody Chick 100% Natural Fully Compostable Diapers Jumbo Box (Junior 26.5 - 55.1 lbs. (96-Count))
*Disposable overnight diapers for heavy wetters Seventh Gen. - Baby Overnight Diapers, Stage 4, 24 Count (Pack of 4) = 96 total diapers
*Triple Paste Diaper Ointment for use with disposablesTriple Paste Medicated Ointment for Diaper Rash, 16-Ounce

There are a number of items that we have tried, but did not find useful and either returned, donated or gave away. There was quite a bit of trial and error and research that went into the above system. I have only listed the items that work beautifully and will simplify your cloth diapering efforts.

I will jump right in with some gripes.
Cloth diapering is not cheap and despite industry claims, I have not realized cost savings in the first year (as compared to buying premium disposable diapers at Costco). When you add up the cost of the diapers and accessories, the cost of your time doing all those extra loads of laundry (or paying a diaper service), the cost of the resources to wash and dry all those loads, and the cost of all the special "cloth friendly" accoutrements necessary to care for the diapers without ruining them, it really adds up. If you are going to use the system for more than once child, then you will realize some significant savings (be sure to buy gender-neutral covers if that is your intention). The bigger motivation to cloth diaper (at least for me) is the dramatically reduced environmental impact and health benefits of cloth diapering. Conventional disposable diapers are loaded with toxins that pollute the earth, the workers that produce them and of course, your baby. Not to mention that it takes over 500 years for regular disposables to break down in the environment. That means that your babies dirty diapers will still be polluting the earth when your great, great, great, great grandchildren are born. Talk about a crappy legacy to leave behind!

Now the good stuff.
I chose Grovia because of the convenience and simplicity of the whole system. I knew that if I was going to cloth diaper, it would need to be as simple and easy as possible, even if that meant I would pay a bit more up front. There is a massive amount of information about cloth diapering on the net and much of it is overly complicated. Cloth diapering does not have to be complicated. You can simplify things quite a bit by sticking with one brand/ one system.

Having said that, the grovia system was too big, hard and bulky for our 10 pound newborn (and Grovia products are pretty trim compared to most cloth diapering systems) so we ended up using disposables for the first couple of months. It would look like he was sleeping on an incline because the grovia insert was so thick under his bum. Other cloth brands have special newborn sized diapers and inserts, but I chose not to invest the money for newborn cloth diapers that would only be used for a short time. We used Broody Chick 100% compostable disposable diapers for that period and the added convenience of the disposables was great during that initial difficult phase. We had some minor problems with diaper rash during that time, but the Triple Paste always cleared it up in a day (then we would go back to the Grovia Magic Stick).

I breastfeed our baby so the diaper changes are more frequent (than formula fed babies). I wanted enough diapers to last three days. I did not want to do an extra load of laundry every two days. I kept adding covers, inserts and wipes until I had enough to last three days, hence the numbers above. If I had known in the beginning how many covers and inserts were going to be required, I would have saved some money by buying some of the larger Grovia combo deals.

The 30 liter Brabantia garbage pail is all steel, easy to clean and self sealing (no stink outside the pail) and the perfect size for three days worth of Grovia wipes, covers, inserts and changing pads. It has an inner plastic pail with handle that makes trips to the laundry room a breeze. The Ubbi cloth pail liners are the only ones that fit the Brabantia pail snugly and I turn them inside out and wash them right along with the diapers. I have two so they can be rotated. For anyone wondering, the dimensions on the Ubbi cloth pail liner are 33x17 (and are NOT the size of a typical "tall" kitchen garbage bag).

I primarily used the 6 velcro covers (more convenient than snaps) through 11 months when our baby was finally able to figure out how to open them (and they are pretty worn looking velcro tabs at this point). Then I transitioned into primarily using the 9 snap covers and keeping the velcro covers only as backup. Grovia has a service that will remove the velcro tabs and replace with snaps for $5 a diaper. I wish they would offer a service to just replace the worn looking velcro, but I am happy with the durability of ALL the covers. They have held up better than I expected.

Our son gets a lot of time in nature. We don't worry about his clothing or diapers getting dirty if he is having a great experience. The darker colors and the patterned shells hide dirt and grass soiling better than the light solid shells. The gentle cloth diaper detergents aren't great at getting out dirt or grass stains either. If I had to do it over, I would not buy any light colored solid covers.

We bought some Grovia Booster pads to try before we went to the disposable overnight diapers, but our guy would soak through the regular "Soaker Pad" and the Booster Pad and wake up with wet clothes and bedding. There just wasn't enough absorbency for our breast fed baby. Changing him several times a night just added to the stress of those early days so we transitioned over to the disposables for night time and have been happy with that choice ever since.

The Grovia Magic Stick is truly brilliant! It is all natural, effective and does not require you to dip your fingers into diaper ointment while wrangling a diaper onto a squirming baby several times a day! It also does not adversely affect the absorbency of cloth diapers they way regular ointments do. Do not use the Triple paste (or any regular diaper ointment) with your cloth diapers. The waxes and petroleum oils in regular diaper ointments coat the cotton fibers and prevent them from absorbing liquids. We tried several top rated "natural" ointments for our occasional diaper rash, but Triple Paste worked where the natural ones failed. We used it sparingly whenever bumps appeared (which was pretty rare compared to our disposable diapering friends).

Grovia wipes are fantastic. I wrote a separate review on these that compared the three types of wipes I bought. Grovia wipes were the best of the three and they work with the Prince Lionheart wipe warmer perfectly.

The Prince Lionheart Premium Wipe Warmer is the perfect size for the Grovia wipes and will hold a stack of about 15 wipes folded in half and stacked. We load a stack of wipes and then fill the wipe warmer with water from the tap. We do not use any "wipe solution" and we have had very little problem with diaper rash. 95% of the world cleans their bums with water alone (no paper) and it works beautifully. We stopped using the warming function after 5 months and baby does not mind the cool wipes at all. At this point, it is basically a nice looking/ easy to sanitize box to keep the wipes moist on the counter.

Little Potty. We started our son on his Little Potty when he was about three months old. Whenever he would cue that he was "going", we would take him to his potty to go. Now at 12 months, we take him to his potty after every meal and when he cues and he uses the potty pretty consistently. It is easier to dump and rinse the little potty contents into the toilet than it is to change and wash his diapers so we encourage him to use his little potty as much as possible. He gets lots of positive feedback and interaction when he "makes it" to the potty so he enjoys using it. At 12 months, he is very comfortable sitting on a potty to pee or poo. We keep the Little Potty on the bathroom counter and stand with him while he is sitting on it. I have seen lots of people start out with the Little Potty on the floor, but if you have the counter space in your bathroom, this will save your knees and back and make it easier for you to interact with your baby while on the potty. In another month or two, we will transition it to the floor for his use.

Grovia Little Bubbles diaper detergent. I have used the 5 top rated diaper detergents and honestly, I can't tell a lick of difference between them. I do a prewash with 1/2 indicated amount detergent and then a full wash with extra rinse using the full amount (per detergent instructions) of detergent. The most important thing is that you use a detergent designed for cloth diapers. Regular detergents have enzymes that will deteriorate the waterproof barriers in your cloth diapers. Cloth diaper detergents also do not have the dyes and perfumes that can irritate baby skin. I am currently using Grovia detergent and it is at least as good as the other top rated diaper detergents. I can't say that it is anymore effective at removing stains than the others. My diaper inserts are stained, but they are clean, soft and have no smell whatsoever when they come out of the dryer. I have always used cloth diaper detergent and I have never had to "strip" them. I use 14 ounces of wool dryer balls to dry my inserts and wipes. The dryer balls shaved ten minutes off the 80 minute dry cycle required to get a load of diapers dry. It takes only 70 minutes with the added wool balls.

Bum Genius Diaper Sprayer. We bought this, but after reading stories of flimsy parts and flooding disasters, we replaced the hose and a couple other flimsy parts with higher quality metal plumbing pieces and have not had any problems for the past year.

Carter's Keep Me Dry FLannel Bassinet Pad. This is my mid layer on the changing pad. I have three layers on my changing pad at all times. Bottom Layer; changing pad cover that came with the pad Middle layer; Carter's Keep my Dry pad Top Layer; Cotton washcloth. The washcloth gets changed about twice per day and does the heavy work in this set up. It catches all the drips, smudges and bits that accompany diaper changing. The Carters catch everything else and help keep the diaper pad cover looking fresh. It gets changed about once per week. The changing pad cover itself gets changed about once per month. This three layer set up makes it easy and fastto keep the changing pad clean and fresh. All of these layers get tossed into the cloth diaper bin and are washed right along with my cloth diapers.

I will come back from time to time to update this review. I hope it was helpful for those trying to wade through all the cloth diapering info out there and find a simple, but effective set up.

Thanks for reading! I hope this review was helpful.

The Prince Lionheart Premium Wipe Warmer is the perfect size for the Grovia wipes and will hold a stack of about 15 wipes folded in half and stacked. We load a stack of wipes and then fill the wipe warmer with water from the tap. We do not use any "wipe solution" and we have had very little problem with diaper rash. 95% of the world cleans their bums with water alone (no paper) and it works beautifully. We stopped using the warming function after 5 months and baby does not mind the cool wipes at all. At this point, it is basically a nice looking/ easy to sanitize box to keep the wipes moist on the counter.

Little Potty. We started our son on his Little Potty when he was about three months old. Whenever he would cue that he was "going", we would take him to his potty to go. Now at 12 months, we take him to his potty after every meal and when he cues and he uses the potty pretty consistently. It is easier to dump and rinse the little potty contents into the toilet than it is to change and wash his diapers so we encourage him to use his little potty as much as possible. He gets lots of positive feedback and interaction when he "makes it" to the potty so he enjoys using it. At 12 months, he is very comfortable sitting on a potty to pee or poo. We keep the Little Potty on the bathroom counter and stand with him while he is sitting on it. I have seen lots of people start out with the Little Potty on the floor, but if you have the counter space in your bathroom, this will save your knees and back and make it easier for you to interact with your baby while on the potty. In another month or two, we will transition it to the floor for his use.

Grovia Little Bubbles diaper detergent. I have used the 5 top rated diaper detergents and honestly, I can't tell a lick of difference between them. I do a prewash with 1/2 indicated amount detergent and then a full wash with extra rinse using the full amount (per detergent instructions) of detergent. The most important thing is that you use a detergent designed for cloth diapers. Regular detergents have enzymes that will deteriorate the waterproof barriers in your cloth diapers. Cloth diaper detergents also do not have the dyes and perfumes that can irritate baby skin. I am currently using Grovia detergent and it is at least as good as the other top rated diaper detergents. I can't say that it is anymore effective at removing stains than the others. My diaper inserts are stained, but they are clean, soft and have no smell whatsoever when they come out of the dryer. I have always used cloth diaper detergent and I have never had to "strip" them. I use 14 ounces of wool dryer balls to dry my inserts and wipes. The dryer balls shaved ten minutes off the 80 minute dry cycle required to get a load of diapers dry. It takes only 70 minutes with the added wool balls.

Bum Genius Diaper Sprayer. We bought this, but after reading stories of flimsy parts and flooding disasters, we replaced the hose and a couple other flimsy parts with higher quality metal plumbing pieces and have not had any problems for the past year.

Carter's Keep Me Dry FLannel Bassinet Pad. This is my mid layer on the changing pad. I have three layers on my changing pad at all times. Bottom Layer; changing pad cover that came with the pad Middle layer; Carter's Keep my Dry pad Top Layer; Cotton washcloth. The washcloth gets changed about twice per day and does the heavy work in this set up. It catches all the drips, smudges and bits that accompany diaper changing. The Carters catch everything else and help keep the diaper pad cover looking fresh. It gets changed about once per week. The changing pad cover itself gets changed about once per month. This three layer set up makes it easy and fastto keep the changing pad clean and fresh. All of these layers get tossed into the cloth diaper bin and are washed right along with my cloth diapers.

I will come back from time to time to update this review. I hope it was helpful for those trying to wade through all the cloth diapering info out there and find a simple, but effective set up.

Thanks for reading! I hope this review was helpful.

After extensive research and even a class on cloth diapering at the local birth center, I settled on the Grovia system and have not regretted that decision. My son is now 12 months and we have been using Grovia since about 8 weeks. We have lots of cloth diapering friends so I have had the chance to see lots of cloth diapers in action and I am very glad that I invested in grovia to begin with. Here are the products we use, why we like them and (in some cases) how they could be improved. There is a lot of material covered in this review. I am doing it this way because I wish there had been something comprehensive like this on Amazon when I was shopping for my diaper stuff! If you are considering buying a Grovia system, this will be helpful for you. If you are looking for a comprehensive review on just one item, other reviews may be more suitable.

Our total system consists of the following:
Essentials;
*15 All In One shells in a variety of prints and solids = 6 velcro and 9 snap GroVia All-In-One (AIO) Cloth Diaper - Planes
*36 organic inserts GroVia Certified Organic Cotton Soaker Pad 2 Pack
*Magic Stick diaper ointment Magic Stick All Natural Diaper Ointment, Clear, Net Wt. 2 oz.
*Brabantia Pedal BinPedal Bin, 30 Litre (White) (25.98"H x 11.54"W x 15.55"D)
*36 Grovia Wipes GroVia Cotton Cloth Wipes, 12 count
*Diaper Detergent Tiny Bubbles Detergent, Net Wt. 60 oz., Yellow Box

Non-essentials, we use these items for convenience,
*Diaper scraper Make My Day Two Birds Silicone Spatula and Scraper Set, Black
*Diaper sprayer bumGenius Diaper Sprayer
*Wipe Warmer (perfectly fits grovia wipes) Prince Lionheart Premium Wipe Warmer
*2 waterproof changing pad covers Carters Keep Me Dry Flannel Bassinet Pad, Ecru
*24 washcloths (for top layer of changing pad)Martex Hospitality Washcloths - 24-Pack (White)
*1 little pottyBABYBJÖRN Little Potty - White
*Disposable Biodegradable diapers for backup and travel Broody Chick 100% Natural Fully Compostable Diapers Jumbo Box (Junior 26.5 - 55.1 lbs. (96-Count))
*Disposable overnight diapers for heavy wetters Seventh Gen. - Baby Overnight Diapers, Stage 4, 24 Count (Pack of 4) = 96 total diapers
*Triple Paste Diaper Ointment for use with disposablesTriple Paste Medicated Ointment for Diaper Rash, 16-Ounce

There are a number of items that we have tried, but did not find useful and either returned, donated or gave away. There was quite a bit of trial and error and research that went into the above system. I have only listed the items that work beautifully and will simplify your cloth diapering efforts.

I will jump right in with some gripes.
Cloth diapering is not cheap and despite industry claims, I have not realized cost savings in the first year (as compared to buying premium disposable diapers at Costco). When you add up the cost of the diapers and accessories, the cost of your time doing all those extra loads of laundry (or paying a diaper service), the cost of the resources to wash and dry all those loads, and the cost of all the special "cloth friendly" accoutrements necessary to care for the diapers without ruining them, it really adds up. If you are going to use the system for more than once child, then you will realize some significant savings (be sure to buy gender-neutral covers if that is your intention). The bigger motivation to cloth diaper (at least for me) is the dramatically reduced environmental impact and health benefits of cloth diapering. Conventional disposable diapers are loaded with toxins that pollute the earth, the workers that produce them and of course, your baby. Not to mention that it takes over 500 years for regular disposables to break down in the environment. That means that your babies dirty diapers will still be polluting the earth when your great, great, great, great grandchildren are born. Talk about a crappy legacy to leave behind!

Now the good stuff.
I chose Grovia because of the convenience and simplicity of the whole system. I knew that if I was going to cloth diaper, it would need to be as simple and easy as possible, even if that meant I would pay a bit more up front. There is a massive amount of information about cloth diapering on the net and much of it is overly complicated. Cloth diapering does not have to be complicated. You can simplify things quite a bit by sticking with one brand/ one system.

Having said that, the grovia system was too big, hard and bulky for our 10 pound newborn (and Grovia products are pretty trim compared to most cloth diapering systems) so we used disposables for the first couple of months. It would look like he was sleeping on an incline because the grovia insert was so thick under his bum. Other cloth brands have special newborn sized diapers and inserts, but I chose not to invest the money for newborn cloth diapers that would only be used for a short time. We used Broody Chick 100% compostable disposable diapers for that period and the added convenience of the disposables was great during that initial difficult phase. We had some minor problems with diaper rash during that time, but the Triple Paste always cleared it up in a day (then we would go back to the Grovia Magic Stick).

I breastfeed our baby so the diaper changes are more frequent (than formula fed babies). I wanted enough diapers to last three days. I did not want to do an extra load of laundry every two days. I kept adding covers, inserts and wipes until I had enough to last three days, hence the numbers above. If I had known in the beginning how many covers and inserts were going to be required, I would have saved some money by buying some of the larger Grovia combo deals.

The 30 liter Brabantia garbage pail is all steel, easy to clean and self sealing (no stink outside the pail) and the perfect size for three days worth of Grovia wipes, covers, inserts and changing pads. It has an inner plastic pail with handle that makes trips to the laundry room a breeze. I reuse use the plastic liners (that came with the pail) by putting them in the sun and alternating every three days. The sun eliminates all the odor from the liners.

I primarily used the 6 velcro covers (more convenient than snaps) through 11 months when our baby was finally able to figure out how to open them (and they are pretty worn looking velcro tabs at this point). Then I transitioned into primarily using the 9 snap covers and keeping the velcro covers only as backup. Grovia has a service that will remove the velcro tabs and replace with snaps for $6 a diaper. I wish they would offer a service to just replace the velcro, but I am happy with the durability of ALL the covers. They have held up better than I expected.

Our son gets a lot of time in nature. We don't worry about his clothing or diapers getting dirty if he is having a great experience. The darker colors and the patterned shells hide dirt and grass soiling better than the light solid shells. The gentle cloth diaper detergents aren't great at getting out dirt or grass stains either. If I had to do it over, I would not buy any light colored solid covers.

We bought some Grovia Booster pads to try before we went to the disposable overnight diapers, but our guy would soak through the regular "Soaker Pad" and the Booster Pad and wake up with wet clothes and bedding. There just wasn't enough absorbency for our breast fed baby. Changing him several times a night just added to the stress of those early days so we transitioned over to the disposables for night time and have been happy with that choice ever since.

The Grovia Magic Stick is truly brilliant! It is all natural, effective and does not require you to dip your fingers into diaper ointment while wrangling a diaper onto a squirming baby several times a day! It also does not adversely affect the absorbency of cloth diapers they way regular ointments do. Do not use the Triple paste (or any regular diaper ointment) with your cloth diapers. The waxes and petroleum oils in regular diaper ointments coat the cotton fibers and prevent them from absorbing liquids. We tried several top rated "natural" ointments for our occasional diaper rash, but Triple Paste worked where the natural ones failed. We used it sparingly whenever bumps appeared (which was pretty rare compared to our disposable diapering friends).

Grovia wipes are fantastic. I wrote a separate review on these that compared the three types of wipes I bought. Grovia wipes were the best of the three and they work with the Prince Lionheart wipe warmer perfectly.

The Prince Lionheart Premium Wipe Warmer is the perfect size for the Grovia wipes and will hold a stack of about 15 wipes folded in half and stacked. We load a stack of wipes and then fill the wipe warmer with water from the tap. We do not use any "wipe solution" and we have had very little problem with diaper rash. 95% of the world cleans their bums with water alone (no paper) and it works beautifully. We stopped using the warming function after 5 months and baby does not mind the cool wipes at all. At this point, it is basically a nice looking/ easy to sanitize box to keep the wipes moist on the counter.

He gets lots of positive feedback and interaction when he "makes it" to the potty so he enjoys using it. At 12 months, he is very comfortable sitting on a potty to pee or poo. We keep the Little Potty on the bathroom counter and stand with him while he is sitting on it. I have seen lots of people start out with the Little Potty on the floor, but if you have the counter space in your bathroom, this will save your knees and back and make it easier for you to interact with your baby while on the potty. In another month or two, we will transition it to the floor for his use.

The most important thing is that you use a detergent designed for cloth diapers. Regular detergents have enzymes that will deteriorate the waterproof barriers in your cloth diapers. Cloth diaper detergents also do not have the dyes and perfumes that can irritate baby skin. I am currently using Grovia detergent and it is at least as good as the other top rated diaper detergents. I can't say that it is anymore effective at removing stains than the others. My diaper inserts are stained, but they are clean, soft and have no smell whatsoever when they come out of the dryer. I have always used cloth diaper detergent and I have never had to "strip" them. I use 14 ounces of wool dryer balls to dry my inserts and wipes. The dryer balls shaved ten minutes off the 80 minute dry cycle required to get a load of diapers dry. It takes only 70 minutes with the added wool balls.

Bum Genius Diaper Sprayer. We bought this, but after reading stories of flimsy parts and flooding disasters, we replaced the hose and a couple other flimsy parts with higher quality metal plumbing pieces and have not had any problems for the past year.

top_rant.text:
I purchased this car seat at the military exchange in Europe - so options were limited to what was on hand, and technically while we could have purchased and returned in new condition within 90 days, there is no real option to try before you buy! With that said, we didn't want to ship anything over to Germany because of the time and cost just to get it here, and the hassle to send it back (and cost) if we didn't like it! So needless to say our experience with the Chicco KeyFit and the other customer reviews plus checking it out in store were the reasons we gave this a try without trying any other seats.

I have a 2012 Volvo XC60 and my husband has an older Range Rover - both have the latch system installed in the outer two rear seats. My daughter is small for her age - she will be 2 years in October and is only 23lbs! She outgrew the KeyFit in length as her head was less than an inch from the top of the seat. I really liked that she could stay rear-facing in this for quite some time given expert guidance that children remain rear-facing as long as possible with up to two years the new recommended minimum. Given her size, I am hoping we get closer to three years!

Ease of Installation and Tight Fit - *****(5/5)
This seat is SO easy to install - took me less than 10 minutes and I actually felt REALLY confident that the fit was right and it was secured. The leveling bubble combined with the ability to change the angle of the base made it super easy to install in both of our cars and didn't leave me with the little niggling of doubt like when I installed the KeyFit. This just gave me the confidence to install the seat and to do it right! I haven't tried it forward facing, but in rear facing we each get two angle choices in order to keep the bubble centered where it needs to be given the angle of the actual seats in the car. Granted, I haven't tried it in a vehicle without the latch system and I imagine that it will be challenging the first time at least - but after traveling with our daughter and using loaner car seats or the KeyFit without the latch system, that is just hard no matter how you slice it and not my preferred way to travel. For everyday use, I love that we can remove it and reinstall it right in under 10 minutes. Can't beat that!

Size - **** (4/5)
This seat is big but honestly not that much more cumbersome than the KeyFit when attached to the base - although there is NO handle or other way of grabbing the car seat other than to wrap your arms around it and carry it. I am a small person (5'3'', 135#) and I had no issues picking this up off the floor and carrying to and installing it in the car. And when I installed this after removing the KeyFit, the front passenger seat actually can go back farther with this seat (in rear facing mode) which is welcome news - not that the KeyFit was obnoxious mind you, but every little bit counts. The head rest of the NextFit is larger and extends out more than in the KeyFit, but partly this is apples to oranges given that the NextFit is a convertible seat and must meet two sets of requirements and what works better given the two uses. BUT with that said, this seat puts my daughter up higher than when she was in the KeyFit so now she can actually see out the windows easier even with the head rest. I have nothing to compare this to as I didn't test out any other seats. Neither of our cars allow for latch system use in the middle of the car - so we could easily install two of these car seats on the left and right and might be able to squeeze another person in the middle but that would be tight. It does change how you get the baby in and out especially if you can't open the car door fully. Overall I didn't find that it made it any more difficult than normal.

Harness - height adjustable (no need to re-thread, just pull up/push down) - ***** (5/5)
The fact that you can change the height of the harness for your growing kid without having to dismantle the seat is an AWESOME feature. The KeyFit wasn't too challenging to adjust all things considered, but when living in Europe and having no garage (parked our cars in a large parking garage) means I would have to cart the seat home to make changes as I couldn't mess with it on the ground in the parking garage or on the street! So this feature alone adds points for this carseat! What I don't like is that it can be hard to push the adjustable button down WHILE trying to adjust the belt pathway and headrest up! You are pushing down on the button while pulling the head rest up - picture it.....exactly, can be a little frustrating, but since you don't have to do this often, not a deal breaker....and not enough to take off a full star. Overall, the ability to adjust without a major hassle makes this a great choice.

Harness/Shoulder Pads - overall *** (3/5)
While it should no longer be an issue with the shoulder pads since the company made changes to all seats manufactured after Dec 2013 - given I am stationed in Europe, the only seats they have on hand were made back in Aug 2013 so yes, you guessed it, those AWFUL shoulder pads are still standard. Good news is that I called customer service in the states and they will mail some replacement ones to me. In the mean time, I guess my only option is to cut them out as another customer suggested because they are awful. They make it harder to tighten the belt and given my daughter's petite frame, they fit her like she is a newborn (and no I would not recommend this seat for children under 6 months unless they grow big fast!) meaning it is almost impossible to tighten the belt enough before the shoulder straps are in the way of putting the chest buckle where it needs to be at armpit level. That is in addition to the fact that tightening the overall belt is REALLY HARD!! It was SO easy when we played with it in the house, but once installed in rear facing mode, it is a real challenge - even my husband thinks so and regularly complains about it! I have had the seat for 3 weeks and it is definitely still a challenge. Partly location of the pull tab at the base of the seat, and partly those awful pads - will see once we get the new ones AND have it a while if 'breaking it in' helps as seems to be the concensus. With that said, I will give it a 3 out 5 but keep in mind that the shoulder pads will not be an issue for most all of you given the changes. So once I get the new ones, I will reevaluate and see if a 4 is better warranted! I would have started with a 1 or 2 star rating for the shoulder pads alone given the hassle it creates to get a squirmy not quite two year old into that seat at least twice a day!! BUT since I was already warned about the shoulder pads thanks to other customer reviews and since I couldn't avoid it given my situation AND that I hope it will be remedied soon. I will start with 3 stars and reevaluate in a few weeks with the new pads and more 'break in'!!

Padding/Comfort - ***** (5/5)
This carseat looks and feels like the macdaddy of the car seats. It is soft and plush feeling and when I was messing with it in the house, my daughter climbed inside and was content to hang out for a while given its recliner feel! Some folks are concerned with breathability - and yes, this is a concern, but it is hard to get plush and comfortable AND breathability while meeting safety standards in my opinion. I have nice leather seats in my car and I sweat on hot days even with the AC! Bottom line, not a reason for me to return it given that my daughter would come out of the KeyFit in the winter in Kansas covered in sweat if she was in the seat long enough. Hence why you layer your children's clothing and NEVER put them in bulky clothing in a car seat (primarily for safety reasons but secondly cause you get HOT!) At this point we are in the middle of summer (we are in a hotter part of Germany and have had a couple weeks of steady temps in the low to high 90s although wouldn't compete where there is high levels of humidity and heat), and I haven't felt that she is sweatier than usual. Certainly makes it harder for her to get cold air given the rear-facing nature of the car seat and placement of most back seat AC vents. Obviously if we lived someplace that was consistently hotter and more humid for several months a year, this might be something that would concern me A LOT more.

Harness Buckle - *** (3/5)
The length on the buckle has been a regular comment and I have to agree that I don't like how short this is and where it rests on my daughter's legs. It is too soon to adjust it forward as some folks mentioned, but given how petite she is, I do not know how bigger kids would fare. I understand you can order a longer attachment (and the new models are already manufactured with this longer belt), but kind of a hassle I think. And not sure if that will fix the gripe. After manufacturer changes should be kept to a minimum given the hassle to make the changes at home!

Overall - so far I really like this seat. While I had no major gripes with the KeyFit and we were happy with it from day one, I think the pros of this seat far outweigh the cons. The ability to keep her rear-facing longer is a big enough plus to off-set most of the negatives. Everyone is different and has different cars, etc. and that is why there are so many car seat options on the market!! So my first recommendation is to test out the car seats and find one that works in your car and for you and your child - with that said, however, we really like the NextFit and so far have been more than happy with this purchase.

For the first time with a stroller especially for umbrella strollers, NO BENDING YOUR BACK AT ALL! stand up straight and close the stroller effortlessly! self-stands! the wheels stay facing the ground, wheels do not go any where near the seat fabric! very sanitary!!!!!!!! you know those hard to access baskets with your strollers especially umbrella strollers? these people are so smart they put a vertical zipper on the side of the basket so you can access without lifting either end of the seat (so no need to push baby up when it is in 180 degree recline! the front has an adjustable foot flap and not only can you adjust down or flat, you can flip it up!!!! up I say!!!!!!!!!! and there are zippers to keep it up like a "wall" to protect baby's feet and keep them from falling (great for younger babies)! the number bar swings out, and also can be replaced with snack tray (perfect large size with cup holder and snack holder on tray), and has hidden out of the way buttons! the seat has side "walls" the umbrella stroller I had did not so it looked like my baby was hanging out and about to fall, so baby is protected from the sides! the canopy covers all the way down to your baby's lap! incredible for privacy and naps! pockets on the back so mom can store ll her things! so easy to put together and take apart! heavy duty wheels! not the cheap hard plastic but real wheels! so much to say I can't believe no one has this, I will be uploading a video on youtube there is so much to say! if i could ever promote and stand behind any one product it would be this! i'm in love!!!!!!!!!!! i have never seen anyone with this stroller!

These sheets have been soiled & pee’d on terribly (my oldest who has medical and skeletal deterioration and cannot always tell if she needs to go outside for bathroom relief as the nerves in her lower spine become pinched) and I mean 😳😱 was how I looked when I saw how bad a few of her bathroom accidents became (and remember standing at the washing machine thinking “there’s no way, no way, these sheets will look half way descent again much less nice no matter how I wash them!” Haha, read on...) top_exclaim.text:
I bought this cause I liked the features it showed to have!! I was in need of a thermometer and thought with this app it would be useful for tracking temps and times so when our pediatrician asked I had it ready!
Well I didn’t set it up right away, I thought I could also use it without the app. Wrong!!!! If you do not set up the app the thermometer will not work!!!! So basically if I’m not home no one can use it cause the app is on my phone! I find that to be ridiculous!!!! Good thing I had a spare ear thermometer my husband could use on our daughter while I wasn’t home!
If it’s not near the app the thermometer won’t read, it gives an error message!! Maybe I’m doing something wrong but I doubt it, I set it up correctly and as long as I do it it’ll work, but if my phone isn’t home it won’t!
I think that needs to be re done!!! I feel the thermometer should work regardless of the app, yes the app is useful for storing info but it should be able to function on its own too!!!
Now about the app!!! Here’s where I’m frustrated, anytime you take a temp it saves to an individual profile... fine if you’re using it to track your child’s temp. But let’s say you wanted to check yourself or your husband or a friends child that is over.... you need to create individual profiles for everyone or it’ll save to that one child’s profile!!! Another ridiculous thing!!
I think it reads accurate and fast but I honestly do not like this thermometer!!! For all those reasons above!!!!
I should be able to use this even without the app!!! If there were ever an emergency this is not reliable because no app no work!!! I will continue to keep my ear thermometer on hand... I was hoping to only need this one from now on but that’s not the case!!! Not fully satisfied! Sorry!!
top_exclaim.title: Without app it doesn’t work! Ridiculous!!

top_five_star_obscene.text:
We love this! I had put this item on a wish list for things my mother should have at her house as the grandma babysitter ;). Some friends of hers purchased it for us and it’s been used a couple times already. It is definitely not small, but it is also not hugely cumbersome. Folding it up is pretty easy as long as you don’t forget the release button on the side (smacking my head for the 10 min of our lives we’ll never get back for that one lol), and can be easily thrown in the trunk. My son is 2 months old and fits in there great. It is floor level so they are pretty buried in there if you don’t put something else in. Bc he’s so small and we want to still see him we put the Boppy Lounger in there and it’s a perfect fit. He snoozed in there almost instantly. It’s portability would be 6 stars if they included even a cheapy carrying bag. I’ve gotten total crap from Amazon and it comes with a bag. This is a quality item so it really should have something to go in. We are traveling to FL for Thanksgiving and want to bring this so we are having it packed and shipped. It will be worth it.

Away from portability it is a great item to have overall. Indoor/outdoor usability (bc of the full range cover) makes it even more worth it. It is durable feeling and should not be something that falls apart. I’m not sure how old my son will be when he outgrows it but I imagine we have at least a few months out of it. It also will make a great storage “bin” for his crap when he gets older that he can stand up at and take toys from. The toys that come with it are too small and way too high for anyone to be reaching so I’d suggest getting your own that are longer.

Totally recommend it. Wish it was maybe $10 less.... but.... shoulder shrug. Awesome item!
top_five_star_obscene.title: Love it

top_five_star_obscene.text:
I was pleasantly surprised at how much I like this baby lotion. I have my 3mo old nephew multiple times a week, so I needed to have lotion on hand for this poor kid who takes after his dad and myself with our crappy family skin. I've had multiple forms of eczema since I was a child, and my brother has really bad facial dermatitis, so it's only natural this poor child ended up with crappy skin too.

The set comes with 3 different lotions - lightweight body lotion, diaper cream, and a vitamin cream. It's great to have on hand for yourself or to give away as a gift set to someone expecting.

Lightweight lotion: I really really like this one. It is very lightweight and leaves the skin feeling almost powdery soft. It's not greasy. Applies easily and soaks into the skin perfectly. I also like that it has a very very light scent, so if you have issues with smells, this is definitely the right fit for you. I typically apply this all over baby after his bath.

Vitamin cream: This one is slightly thicker, but also applies easily. It seems to be for more problematic areas, so since my nephew was having issues with really dry skin on his face, I will apply this one to the face after bath time, just to kind of give him a little more hydration to the dryer areas. Again, very light to no scent at all, so you don't have to worry about it on the face and aggravating your senses.

Diaper cream: I'll be honest, I haven't had to use this one yet, so I can't specifically attest to how well it would work to alleviate any kind of diaper rash, but I have to imagine that it would be just as great as the other 2 lotions in this pack as far as smell and feel. It might be just a bit thicker than the other two given the region of where it's applied and the purpose.

I would definitely recommend this set. The lightweight feel and the little to no scent make it a perfect addition to your baby box of essentials, especially if you have a child with any kind of skin conditions or sensitivities.
top_five_star_obscene.title: Lightweight formula and light scent

top_five_star_obscene.text:
So I spent about 4 months researching formula so when the time came that we needed to supplement from formula in place of breast milk, our baby girl would have the best. I couldn't believe the amount of crap that was in similac and enfamil when I started reading the labels. I wanted organic, and the organic similac used cane sugar, which is just down right terrible for your baby's teeth. Also, the chemicals used to extract DHA in those brands disgust me. Baby's Only extracts DHA and ARA from egg protein, which is awesome. It also doesn't contain all the crap that is in the other brands. There are some chemicals that cannot be avoided when using formula, which there is nothing you can do about. However, you can avoid the other chemicals, and that is what Baby's Only does. We have been rotating breast milk and Baby's Only every other feeding, and our baby girl loves it. No problems with anything what so ever. The customer service is also great with the company. I'm so glad I found this product.

So our baby transitioned from breast milk to formula only around month 4, and she is now almost 8 months old. This formula is still proving to be the best. Absolutely no issues with her feeding with this product. I cannot believe how fast she is growing. I swear, once she started using this formula, she started growing like a weed. The doctor says she is extremely healthy and is most certainly getting the best nutrition she can through this product. I am so extremely happy with this product it really is hard to put it into words. So many others have had so many issues with brands like similac and enfamil. I am really surprised this product isn't so much more expensive, since it is so much better.
top_five_star_obscene.title: The best formula
